evidence: The paper ranks features by impurity importance during optimization and removes all clinical features, concluding they "added no additional benefit." This ranking-based feature selection decision is central to the final "combined model" result. However, they validate only the FINAL MODEL's performance on held-out data (AUC 0.78-0.80), not the importance ranking itself. Without corroboration via permutation importance, SHAP, PDP/ALE, or conditional importance, the impurity-importance bias could have inflated variability feature importance (especially across 71 highly correlated metrics) while depressing clinical feature importance, leading to overcorrected feature removal.
